### Abstract

Food waste and byproducts (FWBP) are a global issue impacting economies, resources, and health. Recycling and utilizing these wastes, due to processing and economic constraints, face various challenges. However, valuable components in food waste inspire efficient solutions like active intelligent packaging. Though research on this is booming, its material selectivity, effectiveness, and commercial viability require further analysis. This paper categorizes FWBP and explores their potential for producing packaging from both animal and plant perspectives. In addition, the preparation/fabrication methods of these films/coatings have also been summarized comprehensively, focusing on the advantages and disadvantages of these methods and their commercial adaptability. Finally, the functions of these films/coatings and their ultimate performance in protecting food (meat, dairy products, fruits, and vegetables) are also reviewed systematically. FWBP provide a variety of methods for the application of edible films, including being made into coatings, films, and fibers for food preservation, or extracting active substances directly or indirectly from them (in the form of encapsulation) and adding them to packaging to endow them with functions such as barrier, antibacterial, antioxidant, and pH response. In addition, the casting method is the most commonly used method for producing edible films, but more film production methods (extrusion, electrospinning, 3D printing) need to be tried to make up for the shortcomings of the current methods. Finally, researchers need to conduct more in-depth research on various active compounds from FWBP to achieve better application effects and commercial adaptability.

Abstract
--------

For many years, conventional plastics are manufactured and used for packaging applications in different sectors. As the food industries are increasing, the demand for [packaging material](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/packaging-material "Learn more about packaging material from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is also increasing. Plastics have transformed the food industry to higher levels; however, conventional petroleum-based plastics are non-degradable which has created severe ecological problems to the environment like a threat to aquatic life and degrading [air quality](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/air-quality "Learn more about air quality from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Biodegradable polymers or [biopolymers](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/biopolymer "Learn more about biopolymers from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") emerged as an alternative approach for many industrial applications to control the risk caused by non-biodegradable plastic. According to the type of starting material, they have been categorized as polymers extracted from biomass, synthesized from monomers, and produced from microorganisms. The quality of biopolymers depends on the physical, mechanical, thermal, and barrier properties. The present review highlights the characteristics of various biopolymers and their blends, comparison of properties between non-biodegradable and biopolymers, the market potential for food packaging applications. The review also emphasizes different commercial forms like films, trays, bags, coatings, and foamed products for application as [modified atmosphere packaging](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/food-science/modified-atmosphere-packaging "Learn more about modified atmosphere packaging from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), active packaging, and [edible packaging](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/food-science/edible-film "Learn more about edible packaging from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Different issues affecting market growth like harmful products formed during production and consumer perception have also been discussed. Information on biopolymers is widely scattered over many sources, this article aims to provide an overview of biodegradable polymer packages for food applications.

1\. Introduction
----------------

In today's life, polymers form an integral part of day-to-day life due to their extensive desirable properties and ease in production. The worldwide production of plastics (Thermoplastics, Thermosets, Elastomers, Adhesives, Coatings and Sealants, and PP-Fibers) was approximately 348 million tonnes in 2017 and it reached 359 million tonnes in 2018. The major contributors include Asia (51%), China (30%), Europe (17%), Middle East & Africa (7%) and others ([Europe & EPRO 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2665927121000496#bib37)). India is one of the leading nations for the production and use of plastic. In 2018–2019, Polyethylene (PE) was the most used plastic in India, utilized in the form of films and sheets with over 15 million tonnes of overall plastic production and is expected to increase 24 million tonnes by 2020 ([Aryan et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2665927121000496#bib7)).

Approximately 95–99% of plastic material is manufactured from non-renewable sources (synthetic plastics) by petrochemical industries ([Mangaraj et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2665927121000496#bib89)). Synthetic plastic products have widely used in fields of medical appliances, packaging, building materials, and packaging, etc. 43% of the synthetic polymers produced annually in India is utilized by the packaging sector ([FICCI, 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2665927121000496#bib42)). [Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2665927121000496#fig1) represents different sectors of plastic utilization in India ([Banerjee et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2665927121000496#bib12)).

However synthetic plastic cannot undergo physical, chemical, and biological degradation and finally leads to an increase in waste ([Vert et al., 2002](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2665927121000496#bib156)). Waste creates numerous severe environmental and health-related problems. They accumulate on the streets and roads, choking drain that results in overflowing ([Foolmaun and Ramjeeawon, 2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2665927121000496#bib44)). A large amount of plastic waste is dumped into the ocean and rivers which harms aquatic life. Incineration leads to the release of harmful gases (carbon dioxide, [carbon monoxide](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/carbon-monoxide "Learn more about carbon monoxide from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), chlorine, 1,3-butadiene, furans, amines, dioxin, etc.) that degrades the [air quality](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/air-quality "Learn more about air quality from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and increases the threat of global warming and possess several health concerns ([Smith, 2005](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2665927121000496#bib137)). The increase in the difficulties for disposing of waste and the harmful effects on the environment and public health caused by the non-degradability of many synthetic polymers have increased concerns all over the world to find an alternative material that is environment friendly. ([Luckachan and Pillai, 2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2665927121000496#bib87)). Biodegradable polymers emerged as an alternative approach for many industrial applications to control the risk caused by non-biodegradable plastic.

According to the American Society for Testing and Materials (ASTM), biodegradable plastic is ‘‘a plastic that degrades because of the action of naturally occurring microorganisms such as bacteria, fungi, and algae” ([ASTM, 2004](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2665927121000496#bib8)). They are produced from renewable sources and have similar properties (tensile strength, thermal properties, elongation at break, water vapor transmission rate and oxygen transmission rate) to conventional plastics like PET (polyethylene terephthalate), [PP](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/polypropylene "Learn more about PP from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (polypropylene), PE (polyethylene), etc. ([Kirwan and Strawbridge, 2003](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2665927121000496#bib75)). Water, carbon dioxide, inorganic compounds, or biomass are the major products formed by the decomposition of biodegradable plastics. There is no accumulation of waste which is beneficial for the environment ([Song et al., 2009](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2665927121000496#bib139)). The main application of biodegradable plastics is in food packaging and agricultural sectors. In the food industry packaging performs different functions which are illustrated in [Fig. 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2665927121000496#fig2).

The packaging is an integral part of the production, storage, distribution, preservation, and other unit operations ([Ivankovic et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2665927121000496#bib61)). In recent years, [bioplastics](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/bioplastics "Learn more about bioplastics from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") are used as an alternative approach instead of conventional plastics for many applications. Bioplastic is a plastic of bio-based origin or biodegradable characteristic of a plastic. According to European standard EN 1675 bio-based is defined as “derived from biomass” ([Van den Oever et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2665927121000496#bib155)). Production of bioplastics requires 65% less energy than conventional plastics and also contributes to less production of greenhouse gases ([Ahvenainen, 2003](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2665927121000496#bib3); [Halley, 2002](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2665927121000496#bib55)).

This paper aims to provide critical information on [biopolymers](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/biopolymer "Learn more about biopolymers from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") as their role in [packaging material](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/packaging-material "Learn more about packaging material from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") which is a key innovation that can help in reducing the environmental impact of [plastic pollution](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/plastic-pollution "Learn more about plastic pollution from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages").

### 1.1. Production statistics

In 2019 the market for biodegradable plastic packaging was valued at USD 4.65 billion and by the end of 2025, it is expected to grow at a CAGR of 17.04% reaching a market value of up to 12.06 million. This increase is due to increasing environmental concerns and various government initiatives to reduce plastic waste. The global production of bioplastic in 2019 was 2.11 million tonnes and it is expected that by the end of 2024 the production will increase up to 2.43 million tonnes. Non-biodegradable, bio-based plastics which consist of PE (polyethylene), PET (polyethylene terephthalate), and PA (polyamides), altogether make up for over 44% of the global bioplastics production. Biodegradable plastics include [PLA](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/polylactic-acid "Learn more about PLA from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), PHA, starch blends, PBS, PBAT, and others constitute over 55.5% of the global bioplastics production ([Market, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2665927121000496#bib91)). In bioplastics production, the major contributions are seen by Asia (45%), Europe (25%), North America (18%), and South America (12%).

Bioplastics are used in different sectors like packaging, consumer electronics, automotive, building/construction, agriculture/horticulture, coatings, rigid packaging, flexible packaging, and various other sectors. The packaging is the largest field of application more than 53% (1.14 million tonnes) of the total bioplastics produced in 2019. Biodegradable food packaging was the first successfully commercialized bioplastic product that is certified as industrially compostable. Since then, there is a tremendous increase in demand for bioplastics as food packaging. Flexible packaging mainly uses biodegradable polymers and rigid packaging mainly contributes to non-biodegradable packaging. Biodegradable polymers are also used for modified atmospheric storage for different fruits and vegetables (Mangaraj et al., 2018).

### 2.1. Polymers extracted/isolated directly from biomass or natural material

#### 2.1.1. Chitin and chitosan

[Chitin](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/chitin "Learn more about Chitin from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is a linear copolymer with a β-1,4 linkage between N-acetylglucosamine and N-glucosamine. The monomers are randomly arranged through the polymer depending on the processing method. It is abundantly available and considered as amino cellulose. chitin is mainly present in shells of insects, crabs, [shrimps](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/shrimp "Learn more about shrimps from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), etc ([Tokura and Tomura, 2007](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2665927121000496#bib152)). Another source for chitin is from fungi cultivation where protein content ranges from 10 to 15%. The solubility of chitin is very low, so it is usually blended for packaging application. [Chitinase](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/chitinase "Learn more about Chitinase from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") degrades chitin ([Teng et al., 2001](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2665927121000496#bib149)).

Partial N-deacetylation of chitin forms chitosan that is insoluble in water and soluble in very few acidic solutions and has a compact crystalline structure and strong hydrogen bonding ([Park et al., 2001](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2665927121000496#bib110)). Chitosan is degraded by [chitosanase](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/chitosanase "Learn more about chitosanase from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") or [lysozymes](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/lysozyme "Learn more about lysozymes from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Their insolubility in most of the solvents limits the applications of chitin and chitosan. N-carboxymethyl chitosan or N-carboxyethyl chitosan is the modified chitosan prepared for application in different industries.

Both are applied to produce various biodegradable films for packaging, and the largest they use as an [edible coating](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/edible-film "Learn more about edible coating from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") to prolong the shelf-life of fresh fruits and vegetables ([Zhao and Mc Daniel, 2005](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2665927121000496#bib167)). Chitin and chitosan have good antimicrobial properties to a variety of fungi, yeasts, and bacteria found in food.

#### 2.1.2. Starch and cellulose

Starch is composed of [amylopectin](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/amylopectin "Learn more about amylopectin from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (poly-α-1,4-D-glucopyranoside and α-1,6-D-glucopyranoside) and [amylose](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/amylose "Learn more about amylose from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (poly-α-1,4-D-glucopyranoside). It is abundantly available and extracted from wheat, rice, potatoes, and [corn](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/maize "Learn more about corn from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). As the source changes, the content of amylose and amylopectin changes. The elongation and strength increase as the amylose content increases ([Ratnayake et al., 2001](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2665927121000496#bib120)). Starch can either be mixed with various resins as a filler to form blends because at a temp of 150–250 °C the linkage breaks and granules disintegrate ([Angellier et al., 2006](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2665927121000496#bib6)). Starch is mostly used as [thermoplastic](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/thermoplastics "Learn more about thermoplastic from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") starch (TPS). TPS is highly sensitive to humidity and the thermal properties changes with the content of water. TPS or plasticized starch acts as an alternative for synthetic polymers. Recent research development in complete biodegradable “green” composites called bio-composites in which biodegradable polymers are blended with [natural fibers](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/natural-fibre "Learn more about natural fibers from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") that are also biodegradable. Biodegradation of TPS is done through hydrolysis of the acetal linkage. [Amylases](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/amylase "Learn more about Amylases from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") break α-1,4 linkage and [glucosidases](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/glucosidase "Learn more about glucosidases from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") cleave α-1,6 linkage ([Netravali and Chabba, 2003](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2665927121000496#bib106)).

In food packaging applications corn-starch is used as TPS. Examples of some of the commercially available starch and their blends include Ecofram™ from National starch, solanyl™ from Rodenburg biopolymers, Biocool™ from Novamont, Bioplast™ from Biotec, and Plantic™ from Plantic Technologies.

Cellulose is a linear polymer formed from repeating units of [cellobiose](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/cellobiose "Learn more about cellobiose from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). It is crystalline and insoluble in organic solvents. Due to its insolubility and low fluency, it is transformed in different forms for their application. This transformation is achieved by various degrees of substitution. As the substitution degree increases the mechanical properties and degradation rate decreases. [cellulose acetate](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/cellulose-acetate "Learn more about cellulose acetate from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (CA) is one of the important derivatives of cellulose with tensile strength like [polypropylene](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/polypropylene "Learn more about polypropylene from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). High glass transition temperature (Tg) limits the application of CA in thermal processing. Commercially available CA films include Bioceta™ developed by the company Mazzucchelli and EnviroPlastic Z™ developed by the company Planet Polymer. Biodegradation of cellulose is done by bacteria and fungi with enzyme [oxidation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/alpha-oxidation "Learn more about oxidation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") specifically by peroxidases secreted by fungi ([Klemm et al., 2002](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2665927121000496#bib76)). Some of the commercially available cellulose-based polymers include Tenite™ from Eastman, Fasal™ from IFA, and Natureflex™ from UCB.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Casting involves dissolving biodegradable polymers in a solvent, then pouring the mixture into molds to form films. This method allows for precise control over film thickness and properties.
Extrusion involves forcing melted biodegradable polymers through a die to form continuous films. This method is efficient, enabling high-speed production of films with uniform thickness.
Thermocompression uses heat and pressure to bond biodegradable materials into films. This technique enhances mechanical properties and is suitable for producing multilayered structures.
The choice of production method affects the mechanical, barrier, and thermal properties of biodegradable films, influencing their performance in packaging applications.
Efficiency in production methods impacts cost, energy consumption, and scalability, making it essential to optimize techniques for sustainable biodegradable film manufacturing.
